questions.question: Explain the purpose of hashing in the context of digital evidence and the Chain of Custody. (3 marks)
expectedPoints: Hashing creates a unique digital fingerprint (e.g., MD5, SHA-256) of a data set.
This fingerprint is taken after evidence acquisition (forensic imaging) to establish a baseline.
The hash is re-calculated at any point later (e.g., before analysis) to verify the data has not been altered, thus proving integrity.

questions.question: Discuss the major factors a digital forensic examiner must consider to ensure their findings will be admissible in court under the Daubert standard. (8 marks)
expectedPoints: Use of Generally Accepted Tools: Employing forensic tools (e.g., FTK, EnCase, Autopsy) that are widely accepted and validated within the digital forensics community.
Documented Methodology: Following a rigorous, repeatable process (e.g., the ACPO guidelines, NIST guidelines) that can be reviewed by other experts.
Error Rates and Validation: Understanding and being able to articulate the potential error rates of their tools and techniques, and validating results where possible.
Peer Review: The ability to state that their methods are subject to peer review through professional publications and community scrutiny.
Proper Evidence Handling: Maintaining an unbroken chain of custody to prove the evidence presented is the same as what was collected and has not been tampered with.
